item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show Reasoning
Hide Reasoning

The following crosslink is being proposed due to the similarity in nature of SCP-2613 to SCP-265.

Editing this:

These processions are led by a hearse (known as SCP-2613-1) which is joined along its route by a number of vehicles (referred to as SCP-2613-2 instances).

To this:

These processions are led by a hearse (known as SCP-2613-1) which is joined along its route by a number of vehicles (referred to as SCP-2613-2 instances).1

Footnotes
1. Due to the similarity of SCP-2613's effects, and the base components of its anomaly to SCP-265, it is currently theorized that the two anomalies may be distantly related, or derived from another, more centralized anomaly. Further investigations into these similarities are ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to say, I like this suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding axslayer33 here: why only the DC area? Given the generalized (heh) nature of the anomaly- mourners in any uniform, from any branch, from any historical period, escorting any deceased veteran that wouldn't get a funeral otherwise- adding "… but only if you died in DC" clashes pretty hard with the rest of the SCP's "characterization." Or, if we're taking "only in DC" as a given, then how many events can there possibly be in a given period of time?

I found an article saying 212 homeless people died in fiscal year 2015 in New York City. NYC is home to at least 8.5 million people; Washington DC is home to about 0.67 million. If we assume similar density of homeless people and of deaths among homeless people between cities (which I recognize are BIG assumptions, but the best I've got in the absence of more data), that means DC's homeless deaths in FY 2015 would come to about 17. The National Alliance to End Homelessness reports that military veterans comprise about 8.6 percent of the total homeless population. So of those 17 homeless deaths in one year in DC, about 1.4 of them would be veterans.

All that said, I really like this concept and I don't want to change it by removing details about the events, even on the reasonable basis that the Foundation couldn't have seen them often enough to draw conclusions. And I recognize that making the Foundation monitor and covertly interfere with a larger area to keep this under wraps would draw attention away from the focus of the article.

+ So here's my recommendation...
- Feel free to use as much or as little as you like.

The procession only appears in DC. Its appearance always coincides with the death of an unidentified and/or homeless US military veteran, and it shows up often enough that the Foundation has observed it on lots of occasions. If the veteran died in DC, everything happens as described in the article- the hearse appears at the scene of the death, it picks up the deceased, and the procession starts from there.

Most of the time, however, the death of the unidentified veteran didn't take place in DC. In those cases, the hearse initially appears as if entering the city from outside (usually via an off-ramp from I-495), already carrying the deceased and followed by some number of mourners- as if the event started in the usual way, but everything outside of DC took place "offscreen" and couldn't be observed by outsiders.

(There may be some correlation between the number of SCP-2613-2 in an initial appearance and the distance traveled before the procession appeared. There may also be a connection between the off-ramp the procession uses and the direction from which it approached the city. Either one would probably help the Foundation figure out where the deceased in a given event came from… which provides some room for really extraordinary variations, such as the nature of a procession for a veteran who died in Hawaii…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems strange to me that they are just driven to the cemetery, with no funeral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for necro-posting; The problem is that tracking the homeless is difficult. If a hobo up and dies, and all anyone knows him as is "Old Joe", then identifying him is difficult (which it mentions is possible, not just complete absence of identification).

Although I do have a problem with the DC area. Unless it's somehow drawing those qualified for the service to the area, there aren't THAT many DC vets unidentifiable. Or is it possible the fact point of origin is unknown, is because it can start anywhere, with D.C. being an endpoint? Lack of origin could be the old "does nothing until un-observed" trick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How exactly have we been unable to identify the majority of these veterans? Their fingerprints/DNA/etc. should be on file for the express purpose of being able to identify their corpse should something bizarre happen to it, and you'd think someone would notice that something is specifically disappearing homeless vets in the DC area.

Needs more explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Technically it only involves those eras during the time the US Government's had a military… and given that in my head there've been close to 100 observed events(and multiple -2 instance in even the smallest events), that wouldn't be too hard to achieve.

That said, I'm glad you like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

Wait.

Your one problem with this thing that reanimates the nameless fallen, and gives them a funeral is that it involves all eras of military development?

That's what's thrown you off?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"have included all eras of service" this seems improbable. That's a lot of eras. 'multiple eras' would be more plausible.

That said, I like the concept. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was expecting the anomaly to involve someone's sedan expanding (seriously, that hearse is freaky looking, is that how they make them these days?), but I like how it ended up. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lo there do I see my father

+1'd at "Bring out your dead" and was not disappointed having read further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This actually plays well into certain urban legends, such as the supposed train staffed by skeletons that ran the country with Lincoln's remains.

Either way, I agree that the article as-is is rather bland… that said, it's also competently written and as such I consider it a good addition to the list as a whole - I fully expect most of the anomalies the Foundation records are ultimately inconsequential odd events like this one.

Hence, I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not enough meat here for my taste.

Yes, it's a culinary joke. Ha.

I like the idea of an anomalous motorcade tending to anonymous soldiers, giving them rest, but it doesn't really have anything to it. I'm not creeped out, or even interested, really. Going to no-vote, for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I certainly enjoy this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are reading it exactly right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So if I'm reading this right, it's a funerary procession of forgotten/abandoned warriors as they attend to one of their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's, overall, a competently written article, and there are no issues with the writing itself.

My downvote comes as a result of the actual idea and article. I went through the whole thing expecting something different to happen, but in the end, it was just kind of a spooky ghost motorcade. The addendum made me expect something else to happen, but it really didn't add a whole lot to the story besides another slightly ominous appearance.

The idea is just played far too straight for me, without any big effect that really made me go "Wow, that was something wholly new or surprising."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I was shopping around 19 for a few days. Also thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did you show it somewhere? I remember seeing something like this.

E: Fixed an SCP-XXXX per te.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So yeah. That's a thing. Thanks to a number of folks for helping me work my way through this idea, especially djkaktus.

Image is creative commons: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:CM-112.jpg
